title: Talking about my life - Boulder, Colorado 10/21

transcript.text: Okay, talking about the Red Zinger.
And I don't know how many kids were showed up in that pack.
I mean, it was maybe like 30 or something, or 25, so I was doing pretty well.
Like, I was ahead of a bunch of kids, or a few kids, so that was cool.
But I remember there were two kids who were in last place, who were like,
And I think they were together, and they were like, you know, and they'd get lapped and stuff.
It was some, like, weird-looking, skinny kid with, like, a crappy bike.
Just, like, a weird, goofy-looking kid with long hair.
And then this fat kid.
And the fat kid was Ryan Gere, who I ended up going to 6th grade with.
Or maybe he wasn't in my, I think he was in the other grade.
But we had, but we shared a choir class.
Because I remember him bragging about how he made the, like, our music teacher cry and then quit his job.
And I remember that.
And this, when we were, like, 9th grade, he was bragging about it.
He bragged about it all the time.
But, like, he, like, I remember we had this, like, music teacher who was, like, he was new.
And he was, like, maybe he was gay.
I don't know.
But he was, like, really, like, he was, like, yay, we're going to do music.
We're going to do this.
I'm so excited.
Let's go.
And then the kids would, like, not be paying attention.
And he'd be, like, wait, you should pay attention to me.
Why are you doing it?
And he, like, started crying one day.
He's, like, you just don't listen to me.
I, you know, I was, like, the coolest kid ever.
I always listened to the kid, to the teacher and wanted to do exactly what the teacher said.
But there were other kids who, like, who were just, like, nasty.
And they'd be, like, what?
Why do we want to listen to that silly song, you know?
Oh, what, now you're crying?
You know, just, like, assholes.
But I wasn't, I wasn't one of those kids.
I was, I kind of felt sorry for the guy.
But, uh, anyways.
Uh, yeah, but anyways, I had, like, junior high with him and high school with him and everything.
And, uh, yeah, he did it in 84 and didn't come back.
And you did that other kid.
But the kid who won, like, it was so much fun because we would, like, you just, you just ride as hard as you can from the beginning.
And, and then, you know, there's always kids in front of you and behind you.
And you try to catch the kid in front of you.
And then you would be in this little group of, like, three or four, you know, and then try to draft off of each other.
And then, like, try and ride away from them and, you know, get over the hill.
And I, you know, I'd be, like, better on the hills because I was lighter.
But then the other kids would, like, catch me on the downhill.
And it was just really fun, man.
And, you know, everybody cheering.
And then there's all these other age groups.
And then, you know, then you have, like, the girls categories.
And it was just, like, this huge event.
It was really fun.
You know, and then in fourth grade, or in 1984 also, that was the year that, that was, like, a big year for sports in America.
Because that was the year that we had the 84 Olympics, Summer Olympics.
And we hosted the 84 Winter Olympics.
And we won, like, every freaking gold medal in the Summer Olympics and in the Winter Olympics.
Like, we won everything.
We won, like, in the Winter Olympics, we won men's downhill, Bill Johnson.
Men's slalom with Phil Mayer.
And men's, like, giant slalom with, like, Steve Mayer, his twin brother.
You know, Scott Hamilton won the, won the freaking figure skating.
Um, and I think, like, didn't we win hockey?
I mean, we won, like, everything.
And then in the Summer Olympics came around.
And we won everything there, too.
And I was following, I followed the Summer and the Winter Olympics.
But I remember the Summer Olympics, um, happened after the Coors Classic.
And for some reason, the Coors Classic happened every year, but I didn't pay attention to it.
I didn't know what it was until 1984, because that's when I was bike racing.
And it happened after the Red Zinger.
So I did the Red Zinger.
I got all excited about bike racing after the Red Zinger.
Oh, yeah.
And, like, the kid who won the Red Zinger, Andy Hooker, man, he, like, won every stage.
He was just, like, blonde kid.
And he won every stage in a solo breakaway.
I guess he'd be, like, with the pack, and then he'd, like, breakaway.
And then the kid, and then there was the other kid, Peter Lynch, who got second.
And then Travis Rosen got third.
And I think, like, Luke Miller was fourth.
I remember the first time I saw Luke Miller, I thought he was a girl, because he had, like, hair down to his butt, and he was really skinny.
It was at, like, some thing where he had to show up before the race, and, like, so they make sure that you know how to ride a bike, you know, so you're not going to crash.
And he showed up there, and I thought he was a girl.
And then I saw him line up against me, and I was like, oh, shit, it's that kid.
Oh, my God, it's a boy.
But, um, yeah, and, um, so I was like, I was like, fuck yeah, next year I'm going to win this.
I want to be like Andy Hooker.
I want to fucking kick ass next year and train my ass off.
So I got all into biking, and then they had the Coors Classic, which was, like, the biggest stage race in all of America.
And, like, a lot of the stages were right in Boulder.
You had, like, a, you had a, uh, uh, uh, Criterium in North Boulder Park that had, like, hundreds of thousands of, of, of, of, of spectators watching it.
And the Coors Pack was huge, and they went so fast.
And, and I remember we went, we saw the, um, the, the Prologue Time Trial of the 84 Coors Classic, which was from Chautauqua Park, and it went up Flagstaff Mountain.
And, um, and it was only, like, a couple blocks up from where my house was.
So we went up there, and we watched it, and there was the announcer there, and all the excitement.
And you saw the guy shoot off the starting block, and some guy, he was like, yeah, and look, this guy, the German guy, or whatever, he's got a $2,000 bike.
Isn't that amazing?
People were like, whoa, $2,000 bike, wow.
And somebody was like, and it's Alexi Graywall.
Oh, I guess he was, like, a big star then, because they were like, here goes Alexi Graywall.
That was, like, one of the first names, I remember.
And then it was like, and here, and Ron Kiefel, and, like, Ron Kiefel won this, the Prologue.
Yeah, and so we watched the, we watched the Coors, and, like, Alexi Graywall was winning the Coors.
And, um, but then he got kicked out, because he had, like, asthma medication or something that had some kind of, like, a stimulant in it.
And they said it was drugs.
So they kicked him out of the, of the, uh, Coors.
So the guy who was second ended up winning the Coors, who was, uh, Doug Shapiro, who was another American.
And, uh, yeah, and I remember they, like, Alexi Graywall was all bummed, because he couldn't, he wasn't going to be able to race the Olympics.
But then they let him race it in, like, the last minute.
And, yeah, I think I remember watching the Olympics on TV, and then Alexi Graywall won the Olympics.
He out-sprinted, like, Steve Bauer, who was supposed to be, like, the best sprinter in the pack, along with Davis Finney.
And Davis Finney, I think, won the, the North Boulder Mall Criterium.
Those were, like, the names that I remember.
And then, like, yeah, Ron Kiefel.
And then the, Roy Nickman was a big deal back then, because he was, like, this 18-year-old kid who was, like, racing with the big guys.
And, like, keeping up with them and stuff.
And he was supposed to be, like, the next, like, badass.
And then we watched the Tour de France.
That was the year I became acquainted with what the Tour de France was.
And the guy who won that year was, like, this blonde dude, Laurent Fignon.
And my dad was, like, yeah.
And so my dad, my parents were starting to get into it, too, like, become fans.
And my dad's, like, yeah, Bernard Hinault, man.
He's the baddest ass.
Won it, like, four times.
And I was, like, oh, yeah.
And I thought Bernard Hinault was Laurent Fignon, because Laurent Fignon won it that year.
But then the next year I realized that Bernard Hinault was this other dude.
But, yeah, but, like, my parents, they drive us up to, like, watch the Coors stages up in, like, Vail and, like, you know, all the other stages around.
So, like, the Morville Bismarck we watch and stuff, which was right outside of Boulder.
So it was fun.
And so then after fifth grade, I went to fifth grade at Waldorf, a private school, and I rode my bike.
And, okay, so then I had a new bike.
I didn't have that push anymore.
I got a top-of-the-line bike the next year.
It was, like, this, you know, top-of-the-line steel frame, like, what was it called?
It was the Schwinn Peloton.
And it had, like, you know, it had shifters on the bottom.
And it was basically the best.
It was, like, a badass bike.
And I bought it with my own money.
It was, like, 600 bucks, and I bought it because I was, like, mowing lawns from all the neighborhood houses, you know, while I was in fourth grade and maybe the beginning of fifth grade.
And, yeah, I bought it all with my own money.
And I rode my bike to school every day in Waldorf, which was, like, not close to our house.
It was, like, kind of on the other side of town.
It was, like, near – it was on Foothills Parkway and Baseline.
And my house was on Baseline and Broadway.
So it was, like, a few miles away.
And then I'd do – and I'd be at class, and then I brought my bike shorts with me.
And then after class, I'd put my pants, my clothes pants, like, pack them in, like, this bag under my seat.
And then I'd do two laps at the Morville Bismarck and then go home, you know, like, riding as hard as I could.
And so that was 37 miles that I'd ride a day because it was 13 miles a lap around Morville Bismarck.
And then – so that's 26 miles.
And then – so, yeah, it was, like, five and a half miles to school or something.
And then I'd have to ride home from the end of the Morville Bismarck.
So – but I did that every single day.
Like, I pretty much – six days a week.
Like, every day after school, you know, I mean, sometimes I'd miss, but pretty much every day I did that.
So I was riding, like, 210 miles every single week when I was 10 – when I was 11,
Which is probably more riding than any of the other kids my age were doing.
And I did, like, a training ride.
At least I remember one training ride I did with my teammate from the year before, Chad, I think his name was.
And he was going to race it the next year, but in the age group above.
And I think he got sixth overall.
But he – he went on a ride with me, and his mom actually chaperoned to him.
He rode to my house, but his mother came with him in the car because I guess she wanted to make it safe or something.
But we did a lap in the Morville Bismarck, and I'm like, yeah, I'm going to win.
He's like, no, you're not.
I was like, what do you mean?
He's like, dude, there's kids.
There's kids who beat you last year who were going to – who were 10 years old.
And I was like, really?
I was like, I don't know any of them.
He's like, yeah, dude.
I was like, oh.
Well, anyways, I'm going to kick ass.
He's like, cool, man.
And so, yeah, we did that.
And, yeah, and then the 85 Red Zinger came around.
Only this year we had even more races.
We had like two warm-up races.
We had – there was one in Fort Collins and one in Denver.
And the one in Fort Collins was a three-stage race.
It was a time trial and then a road race and then a criterium.
And then Denver was like the same thing.
And I think the one in Fort Collins was first.
And like the father of one of the kids who raced who was in my age group, Eric Owen, his dad, Fred Owen, was the director of that one.
And then Beeler, I think the father of some kid named Jeff Beeler who was actually like a year or two older than me racing.
His dad, like Greg Beeler or something, was the director of the one in Denver.
And so, yeah, we did the time trial and I got like fifth or something.
And then we did a road race.
And I remember Eric Owen was one of my teammates.
And I remember going to Eric Owen.
I'm like, yeah, dude, we're going to do a breakaway around this corner right before the finish.
He's like, okay.
But, yeah, we do the road race.
And, like, I sprinted.
There was the, there was the preem and I sprinted ahead really fast.
Like before anybody else sprinted.
And because I started the sprint really early.
And some kid said to some other kid, like, watch out.
Like, let's get the tacking.
Anyways, because I sprinted first, I won the preem.
And I won some, like, like handlebar tape.
But then, like, when the attack happened, pretty much about where I predicted, I wasn't the one doing the attack.
And I couldn't hang on.
And so I ended up getting, like, fifth.
I was fourth overall.
And then there was this other kid with glasses.
I think it was Jonathan Murley.
But he was fifth.
And we were in the criterium.
And I got dropped from the main group of, like, four or three kids.
And then there was me.
And then behind me was him.
And for some stupid ass fucking reason, I stopped.
I actually stopped and put my foot down.
Waited for him to catch me.
So that we could work together and catch the other guys in front.
I don't know what the hell I was thinking.
We didn't catch the guys in front.
And then he out sprinted me.
And then he ended up beating me overall because of that.
So I was like, I am the biggest moron.
I could have just kept riding.
And I would have beaten that motherfucker.
But anyways.
Then the Red Zinger happened.
Oh, and then the Denver race happened.
And some kid named Mike Constantino won that.
And then the Boulder race happened.
And we had this time trial from the hill up to Chautauqua.
And I rode that one.
That was, like, perfectly suited to me because I was, like, a really little kid.
Tiny little kid.
I was, like, 80 pounds or something.
Or less.
I mean, I was tiny.
So I just hammered my brains out like harder than I had ever ridden in my entire life.
Because I knew I was strong.
And that just kind of gave me extra motivation to hammer.
And I ended up getting third.
But this other kid won who hadn't shown up for the first race.
It was for the Fort Collins race or the Denver race.
Chris Weary.
He beat me by six seconds.
And I think Mike Constantino got second.
He beat me by three seconds.
And then I beat all the other kids.
And then we had this other race, like, right around where Waldorf was.
Right around our school criterium.
And I got beat on that one.
I think I got dropped from the pack.
And then the third race was Boulder Mall.
But I was still third place overall for some reason.
But then I got dropped from the pack in that criterium.
So then I fell back to eighth.
And that's where I stayed for the rest of the race.
Because, like, I would, I just couldn't, when there was a speed surge, I just couldn't go.
I was too little.
Kids would just sprint ahead.
And then I was dropped.
And then I was all by myself.
But I managed to stay with the lead pack on the Longmont race.
Oh, my God.
We're out of time again?
Jesus Christ.
I'll see you guys later.
Hey.
Wait.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
